preFix.title: Designing a travel journal spread
preFix.subtitle: Laying out a single location spread to hold sketches, notes, tickets, and impressions from a trip
preFix.excerpt: A travel journal spread captures a place or day in a way that can be revisited later. The challenge is combining different types of content: quick sketches, factual notes, ephemera like tickets and maps, and personal impressions. A flexible grid structure holds all of these without the spread looking cluttered.
preFix.sourceNotes: Common journalling practice for travel documentation. No single primary source.

content.text: A travel spread works best when it is assembled in two stages: quick capture on the day and finish-and-stick when you have more time. Trying to compose a polished spread at the moment of experience slows both the journalling and the experience. Capture notes and sketches in the moment, attach
and add watercolour washes and finishing detail when you have space and light.

items.name: Watercolour paint, half-pan set (optional, for washes)

content.text: A two-page A5 spread can be split into three zones. Left page: a large sketch area (half the page) and a notes area (the other half). Right page: an ephemera zone for tickets, maps, and smaller sketches. Sketch the layout in pencil before committing to it.
At the top of the left page, write a header: place name and date, in larger lettering. This anchors the spread to a specific point in time and geography.

Reserve the largest zone for the main sketch or view. This should be the visual anchor: a skyline, a building facade, a market scene. Fill it in pencil on the day; add watercolour wash or ink detail later.
: a cup, a doorway, a signage detail, a local flower. These require only two or three minutes each but build a more complete picture than a single large view.

Write travel notes in a compact hand in the remaining space. Record specific, sensory details rather than general impressions: the smell of a market, the exact colour of stone in afternoon sun, the noise level of a square. Specific details are more retrievable than general statements.

Attach ephemera with washi tape temporarily on the day (so you can reposition) and fix permanently with PVA when you settle the final layout. Fold over-size items: a folded A5 map tucked behind a flap reads as one element in the spread and opens for full viewing.

Travel journals take water from condensation, food, and rain, and they are used on uneven surfaces. A lighter paper weight (90 gsm) is easier to fold and carry; a heavier weight (130 gsm) tolerates more water and ink without cockle. Watercolour work directly in the journal requires at least 130 gsm and benefit from paper that is lightly sized. If the journal must be waterproof, work on separate loose sheets and stick them in on return.
